message.content:
The air in Mr. Thorne’s shop was thick with the scent of old paper, brass polish, and dust motes dancing in the slanted afternoon light. The room was a cacophony of ticks, tocks, chimes, and whirs, a symphony of mechanical hearts beating in slightly different rhythms.

Leo, seven years old and possessing the boundless energy of a spring-loaded toy, pressed his nose against the glass case of a grandfather clock that hadn’t worked since 1924.

"Mr. Thorne?" Leo whispered, though the noise of the shop made whispering somewhat redundant.

Mr. Thorne looked up from his workbench, where he was carefully adjusting the escapement of a pocket watch. His spectacles were perched precariously on the end of his nose, and his hands, stained with oil and age, moved with the precision of a surgeon.

"Yes, Leo?"

"Does time hurt?"

Mr. Thorne paused, his tweezers hovering over a tiny ruby bearing. He blinked slowly, then removed his glasses, cleaning them on his apron. "That is a very strange question for a Tuesday afternoon. Why do you ask?"

Leo pointed a sticky finger at the wall of clocks. "Because you fix them. You make them go again. But my grandma says time never stops. She says it just keeps going whether you want it to or not. So if I break my toy, you can fix it. But if I break a minute... can you fix that too?"

Mr. Thorne set his tweezers down and leaned back in his creaking chair. He gestured for Leo to come closer. Leo scrambled over a pile of spare gears and sat on a stool that was too high for him.

"Come here," Mr. Thorne said softly. He picked up the
